meta.book: Esther (Addition D: Esther Before the King)

preamble.summary: Addition D replaces or dramatically expands the MT's brief account of Esther approaching the king (5:1-2). Where the MT records simply that Esther stood in the court, found favor, and the king extended his scepter, the LXX unfolds a theatrical scene: Esther enters in royal splendor but with a heart frozen in fear, the king's face burns with anger, Esther faints, and 'God changed the spirit of the king to gentleness.' The king leaps from his throne, embraces her, and reassures her.
preamble.remarkable: The phrase 'God changed the spirit of the king to gentleness' is the single most important theological addition in the entire LXX Esther. It answers the question the MT deliberately leaves open: why did the king receive Esther favorably? The MT invites the reader to see providence in coincidence; the LXX states it plainly. Esther's double fainting, the king's alarm, and the tender embrace create an intimacy absent from the formal MT scene.
preamble.friction: This addition has no Hebrew counterpart. Its high emotional register differs markedly from the MT's restrained prose style. Some scholars argue it diminishes Esther's courage by making her faint rather than stand firm. Others see it as heightening her courage — she acts despite overwhelming terror.
preamble.connections: Esther 5:1-2 (MT parallel); Addition C (Esther's preceding prayer); Esther 4:16 ('if I perish, I perish'); Daniel 10:8-12 (fainting before a divine/royal presence); Proverbs 21:1 ('the king's heart is in the hand of the LORD').

verses.text_source: Greek: Καὶ ἐγενήθη ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ
verses.text_reference: On the third day, when she ended her prayer, she took off the garments in which she had worshiped, and arrayed herself in splendid attire.
verses.rendering: On the third day, when she had finished her prayer, she took off the garments of mourning in which she had worshiped and arrayed herself in all her splendor.
translator_notes: The dramatic transition from the ashes of prayer (Addition C) to royal splendor marks one of the most powerful costume changes in all literature.

verses.text_reference: Then, majestically adorned, after invoking the aid of the all-seeing God and Savior, she took her two maids with her.
verses.rendering: Then, having made herself resplendent, after calling upon the all-seeing God and Savior, she took two servant girls with her.

verses.text_source: Greek: τῇ μὲν μιᾷ ἐπηρείδετο ὡς τρυφερευομένη
verses.text_reference: On one she leaned gently for support, while the other followed, carrying her train.
verses.rendering: On one she leaned gently, as though delicately, while the other followed behind, bearing her train.

verses.text_source: Greek: αὐτὴ δὲ ἐρυθριῶσα ἀκμῇ κάλλους αὐτῆς
verses.text_reference: She was radiant with perfect beauty, and she looked happy, as if beloved, but her heart was frozen with fear.
verses.rendering: She was radiant with the perfection of her beauty, and her face was joyful, as one beloved — but her heart was frozen with fear.
translator_notes: The juxtaposition of outward radiance and inner terror is one of the great psychological moments in ancient narrative. Esther's courage is not the absence of fear but action despite it.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ εἰσελθοῦσα πάσας τὰς θύρας
verses.text_reference: When she had gone through all the doors, she stood before the king. He was seated on his royal throne, clothed in the full array of his majesty.
verses.rendering: When she had passed through all the doors, she stood before the king. He was seated on his royal throne, clothed in the full splendor of his majesty,

verses.text_source: Greek: πᾶς χρυσῷ καὶ λίθοις πολυτελέσιν κεκοσμημένος
verses.text_reference: all covered with gold and precious stones. He was most terrifying.
verses.rendering: all adorned with gold and precious stones. He was most terrifying.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἄρας τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ πεπυρωμένον δόξῃ
verses.text_reference: Lifting his face, flushed with splendor, he looked at her in fierce anger. The queen faltered, and turned pale and faint.
verses.rendering: Lifting his face, burning with majesty, he looked at her in fierce anger. And the queen faltered — she turned pale and nearly fainted,

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐπέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν τῆς ἅβρας
verses.text_reference: and collapsed on the head of the maid who went in front of her.
verses.rendering: and she collapsed against the head of the servant girl who walked before her.
translator_notes: Esther faints — a detail entirely absent from the restrained MT account. The LXX dramatizes the mortal danger of approaching the king uninvited.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ μετέβαλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ βασιλέως εἰς πραΰτητα
verses.text_reference: Then God changed the spirit of the king to gentleness, and in alarm he sprang from his throne.
verses.rendering: Then God changed the spirit of the king to gentleness. In alarm he leapt from his throne,
translator_notes: This is the theological climax of Addition D: 'God changed the spirit of the king.' The MT never names God as the agent behind the king's favorable response. The LXX makes divine intervention explicit — this is not political luck but supernatural rescue.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἀνέλαβεν αὐτὴν ἐπὶ τὰς ἀγκάλας αὐτοῦ
verses.text_reference: and took her in his arms until she came to herself. He comforted her with soothing words.
verses.rendering: and he gathered her into his arms until she recovered herself. He comforted her with soothing words.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῇ τί ἐστιν Εσθηρ
verses.text_reference: He said to her, 'What is it, Esther? I am your husband. Take courage.'
verses.rendering: And he said to her, 'What is it, Esther? I am your husband. Take courage.

verses.text_reference: 'You shall not die, for our law applies only to our subjects. Come near.'
verses.rendering: You will not die — our decree is for the common people only. Come near.'
translator_notes: The king's reassurance — 'our law applies only to subjects' — suggests that the queen is exempt from the rule against unauthorized approach. This legal nuance is unique to the LXX.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἄρας τὴν χρυσῆν ῥάβδον ἐπέθηκεν ἐπὶ τὸν τράχηλον αὐτῆς
verses.text_reference: Then he raised the golden scepter and touched it to her neck.
verses.rendering: Then he lifted the golden scepter and laid it on her neck.
translator_notes: In the MT (5:2), the king extends the scepter for Esther to touch. In the LXX, he places it on her neck — a more intimate gesture of royal protection.

verses.text_reference: He embraced her, and said, 'Speak to me.'
verses.rendering: And he embraced her and said, 'Speak to me.'

verses.text_reference: She said to him, 'I saw you, my lord, like an angel of God, and my heart was shaken with fear at your glory.'
verses.rendering: And she said to him, 'I saw you, my lord, as an angel of God, and my heart was troubled by the fear of your glory.

verses.text_reference: 'For you are wonderful, my lord, and your countenance is full of grace.' And while she was speaking, she fainted and fell again.
verses.rendering: For you are wonderful, my lord, and your face is full of grace.' While she was speaking, she fainted once more and fell.
translator_notes: Esther faints a second time — the scene's emotional intensity exceeds anything in the MT. The king and all his servants are alarmed. The addition transforms a brief political encounter into a dramatic rescue narrative.
